behaviors.description: Request to external API includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests request construction — verifying the payload includes all taxonomy labels. A unit test mocking the API and inspecting call arguments directly catches the failure (missing labels in request). No external infrastructure needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API client. Call the classification module with a document. Assert the mocked API was called with the correct taxonomy labels in the request arguments.

behaviors.description: When API returns a label outside the fixed taxonomy, module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value
behaviors.justification: This is a validation/sanitization behavior. Mock the API to return an invalid label (e.g., 'spam'), verify the module returns 'unknown'. No I/O or external dependency needed; logic is deterministic and local.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a label not in the taxonomy. Call the classification module. Assert it returns 'unknown' rather than the raw API response.

behaviors.description: Classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls
behaviors.justification: Most likely tests caching: two calls with the same document should produce the same result and hit the API only once. Mock the API to return a label, call twice, assert identical result and API called once. However, if the intent is to verify the module handles a non-deterministic LLM consistently (real API), Agentic level is required. Ambiguity reflects whether caching or LLM-determinism is the goal.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API to return a fixed label. Call the module twice with the same document. Assert: (1) both calls return the same label, and (2) the API was called exactly once (proving caching). If testing real API non-determinism, use a real API and run multiple times to verify consistency despite variance.

behaviors.description: Number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget
behaviors.justification: Testing token budget requires inspecting the actual request payload sent to the API to count tokens. While you can mock the API (avoiding real network I/O), you need to verify the request construction—checking request size, encoding, and token count—which is beyond a simple unit-level mock. Integration level (mocked API but full request inspection) catches cases where the request payload exceeds budget. Agentic level required only if the budget must account for output tokens from the LLM response, which is non-deterministic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API. Build a typical document and call the module. Intercept the request sent to the API, measure its token count using the same tokenizer the module uses, and assert tokens < budget. Alternatively, with a real API, measure both input and output tokens to verify total consumption.

behaviors.description: When API signals rate limiting (e.g., HTTP 429), module waits and retries instead of returning an error to the caller
behaviors.justification: Retry logic is testable at unit level by mocking both the API and the sleep function. Mock the API to return 429 on first call, then success on second. Mock time.sleep to avoid real delays. Verify the module retried (API called twice) and returned the success result. No real infrastructure needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return HTTP 429 on the first call, then return a valid classification on retry. Mock the time.sleep function to avoid delays in tests. Call the module once. Assert: (1) the API was called at least twice, (2) the module eventually returned a valid result, and (3) it did not immediately error to the caller.
